title: Time Complexity
description: The payoff deferred since Topic 1: how the amount of work an algorithm does grows as input size grows, and the O(1)/O(n)/O(n²) shapes.
objectives: Understand time complexity as a description of growth, not exact runtime
Recognize constant, linear, and quadratic time from code shape
Understand why this matters more as input size grows
Preview Big O notation as the formal language for this

sections.text: 1. Recap: This Was Deferred Since Lesson One
Nearly every earlier lesson included a line like "Time Complexity: Not applicable to our current topic — we will study it properly before using it in DSA." That deferred moment has arrived.
Time complexity describes how the amount of work an algorithm performs grows as the size of its input grows — not an exact time in seconds, but a shape of growth.

Introduction to C++ asked exactly this question early on: "If the input grows from 10 elements to 1,000,000 elements, how does the algorithm's work grow?" Time complexity is the formal answer to that question.
3. Constant Time — Doesn't Grow at All
Whether numbers has 5 elements or 5 million, numbers[0] takes the same single step. This is constant time — the work doesn't depend on input size at all.
Double the size of numbers, and the loop runs roughly twice as many times. The work grows in direct proportion to the input size — this is linear time.

For every single element the outer loop visits, the entire inner loop runs again. With n elements, that's n × n = n² total operations — this grows dramatically faster than linear time. Recall the 2D Arrays nested-loop pattern; the same shape here has a real complexity cost.

At small n, the difference barely matters. At the sizes real DSA problems and interviews often test — thousands or millions of elements — an O(n²) approach can become genuinely too slow to finish in reasonable time, while an O(n) approach handles it easily. This is precisely why time complexity is a central part of coding interviews, not just an academic exercise.
This is only a preview — O(1), O(n), and O(n²) are examples of Big O notation, the formal way of writing these growth shapes, covered fully in the very next topic.

sections.title: Checking one locker vs checking every locker vs checking every pair of lockers
sections.text: Grabbing locker #5 directly takes the same effort no matter how many lockers exist in the building — constant time. Checking every locker one by one to find something takes longer as the building grows — linear time. Comparing every locker against every other locker gets dramatically slower as the building grows — quadratic time, since doubling the lockers roughly quadruples the comparisons.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Thinking time complexity measures exact seconds — it describes growth shape, independent of any specific machine's speed.
Missing a nested loop hiding inside a function call — if a loop calls a function that itself loops over the input, the real complexity may be higher than it looks at first glance.

Assuming a lower time complexity is always better in every real situation — for very small inputs, a 'worse' complexity algorithm can sometimes be faster in practice due to lower overhead, though this rarely dominates the analysis at the level we'll use it.
Confusing time complexity with output size — an algorithm that must print n² pairs cannot be faster than O(n²), regardless of how efficient the underlying logic is.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is time complexity?
questions.answer: A description of how the amount of work an algorithm performs grows as its input size grows, independent of any specific machine's raw speed.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing a single array element by index?
questions.answer: Constant time — it takes the same one step regardless of the array's size.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of a single loop over n elements?
questions.answer: Linear time — the work grows directly proportional to n.

questions.question: Why does a nested loop over the same collection typically indicate quadratic time?
questions.answer: Because the inner loop runs fully for every single step of the outer loop, giving roughly n × n = n² total operations.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Time complexity describes how an algorithm's amount of work grows as its input size grows, not an exact runtime in seconds. Accessing a single array element is constant time, since it doesn't depend on size at all. A single loop over every element is linear time, growing proportionally with input size. A nested loop over the same collection is typically quadratic time, since the inner loop runs fully for every step of the outer one. This distinction matters enormously at scale — an O(n²) algorithm can become impractically slow on large inputs where an O(n) algorithm remains fast, which is why time complexity is central to DSA and coding interviews.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Time Complexity
sections.text: O(1) = grab one locker. O(n) = check every locker once. O(n²) = compare every locker against every other locker. Doubling n barely moves O(1), doubles O(n), quadruples O(n²).
